channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘The Bachelorette’ Took a Risk on Taylor Frankie Paul. It Backfired.
item.description: Ms. Paul’s built-in fan base and viral interpersonal drama presented an opportunity for the long-running reality TV show, until video of a past assault derailed the season.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and John Koblin
item.category: Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)

item.title: BTS’s Comeback Show Was Heavy on Their New Album
item.description: The concert in Seoul’s historic center was a grand homecoming for a pivotal force of South Korean soft power. RM, the group’s leader, performed through an ankle injury.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times

item.title: Chuck Norris: A Life in Pictures
item.description: The actor, known for his skills as a martial artist, often played the warrior onscreen.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Scott Evans Recommends These Books to the Women in His Life
item.description: The “Access Hollywood” and “House Guest” host shares his love of “Jesus Christ Superstar,” Keke Palmer and celebrity memoirs that go deep.
item.dc:creator: Leigh-Ann Jackson

item.title: St. Vincent, Death Cab for Cutie: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: St. Vincent teams with a 60-piece orchestra, and Squarepusher replaces one by playing all the parts himself.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: St. Vincent’s “Live in London!” captures a performance conducted by Jules Buckley.

item.title: BTS Is Back With ‘Arirang,’ but the K-Pop Landscape Has Changed
item.description: The superstar boy band returns after a four-year hiatus on Friday. The genre it helped turn into a global juggernaut has endured some shifts, and minted new stars.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.media:description: BTS onstage at the Grammys in 2022. The group is returning with its first album since that year on Friday.

item.title: Gustavo Dudamel’s Portrait Emerges From a Season of Introductions
item.description: The New York Philharmonic’s next music leader, slowly revealing facets of his personality, used his latest visit to showcase his political conscience.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: The People United Will Never Be Defeated! (Musical Work)

item.title: What the ‘Project Hail Mary’ Directors Learned From a Firing
item.description: Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, best known for animations like the “Spider-Verse” films, took lessons from “Solo: A Star Wars Story,” a project from which they were dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Lord, left, and Christopher Miller. “It was almost contrarian to make a movie that’s so affirming about a topic that is on everyone’s mind, which is this idea that we’re doomed,” Lord said.

item.title: A Painter Faces His Biggest Show, and the Truth About Success
item.description: As Hurvin Anderson prepares for a major retrospective of his work at Tate Britain, he’s unsure how to feel about his achievements.
item.dc:creator: Kadish Morris and Tom Jamieson For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Tom Jamieson for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Saturday Night Live’ Arrives in London
item.description: Britain has a storied history of sketch comedy, but its humor has a very different flavor than the American variety.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:description: The cast of the British version of “Saturday Night Live,” which premieres this weekend and will be available on Peacock in the United States.

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Review: Valerie Cherish vs. the Machine
item.description: In its third season, the HBO satire grows from cringe-comedy character study into a dark satire of labor in the A.I. era.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: Man Arrested After Valuable Glass Artwork Is Shattered at Seattle Museum
item.description: A man was arrested after being accused of damaging an estimated $240,000 of artwork at the Chihuly Garden and Glass.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.category: Chihuly Garden and Glass (Seattle, Wash, Museum)
item.media:credit: Chihuly Studio/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chihuly Garden and Glass in Seattle. Twelve items were reported damaged, each valued at an estimated $20,000.

item.title: ‘Monte Cristo’ Review: A Tale of Betrayal, on Two Counts
item.description: An adaptation has a twist that doesn’t track, and songs that benefit from an excellent cast, including Norm Lewis, Sierra Boggess and Adam Jacobs.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Who Is Antigone? The 2500-Year-Old Rebel With a Cause.
item.description: “Antigone” gave us the original “bad girl,” but its themes go beyond that. How do adaptations keep making Sophocles’ ideas about democracy and theater new?
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Why Are We Obsessed With Antigone?
item.description: Antigone, an ancient Greek play, is being adapted in several theaters across New York City. Our critic Helen Shaw explains why Sophocles’s anti-heroine is such a relevant figure today.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry

item.title: K-Pop’s BTS Returns to the Stage
item.description: The group’s first concert in nearly four years — held in the historic center of Seoul — was heavy on songs from its new album. They also played a few old hits.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times

item.title: The director of the Netflix livestream is a veteran of the Oscars and the Super Bowl.
item.description: Hamish Hamilton, a multiple-time Emmy and BAFTA winner, is directing the Netflix broadcast of BTS’s comeback concert on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Hamish Hamilton, an accomplished television director who specializes in live broadcasts, accepting an Emmy Award in 2024.

item.title: Behind the scenes of the BTS livestream.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, where the concert is taking place on Saturday.

item.title: Why BTS Is Promoting Its New Album, ‘Arirang,’ in Korea Before Hitting the Road
item.description: The last time BTS released an album, it kicked off with appearances in New York before Seoul. This time it’s the other way around.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Benjamin
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: BTS merchandise at a convenient store in Seoul on Saturday.

item.title: What BTS Wore for Its Comeback Concert and Why
item.description: The band’s looks, by the Korean brand Songzio, represented a bigger story about Korean culture and meaning.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: Here’s how America met BTS.
item.description: The South Korean band broke into American pop culture by hitting the talk shows, performing on “Saturday Night Live” and appearing at the White House.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:description: Members of BTS joined the White House press secretary, Karine Jean-Pierre, at the White House, in 2022.

item.title: There was extra security as BTS fans arrived at the concert site.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim

item.title: One reason BTS went on hiatus? To do military service.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: BTS members RM, left, and V in Chuncheon, South Korea, last year, after their release from 18 months of military service.

item.title: ​Gwanghwamun Square, the venue of the show, has long attracted crowds.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: A protest against then President Yoon Suk Yeol of South Korea in Gwanghwamun Square in 2024.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young, Yan Zhuang, Jeff Benjamin and Max Kim

item.title: Art Basel Hong Kong: Injecting Energy Into the Art Fair Experience
item.description: A four-person team has shaped the Encounters section of Art Basel Hong Kong, organizing it around the elements of water, fire, earth and ether.
item.dc:creator: Bonnie Tsui

item.title: At Hong Kong Art Basel, an Exhibition of Works by El Anatsui
item.description: The artist El Anatsui has returned to his roots in Ghana and is showing in Hong Kong during Art Basel.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: A Navy Veteran Finds His Niche in the Art World
item.description: It took courage for Walter Price to move to New York and pursue a career as a painter, but he says his dream has come true.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Contemporary painter Walter Price at his studio in Bushwick, Brooklyn.

item.title: Nicholas Brendon, Beloved Sidekick on ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer,’ Dies at 54
item.description: He played the part of Xander Harris, one of Buffy’s closest friends, on the hit television show about a teenage girl who protects the world from monsters.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Nicholas Brendon, who played the lovable sidekick on the hit show “Buffy the Vampire Slayer,” has died.

item.title: Don DeLillo’s Hockey Novel, ‘Amazons,’ Will Return to Stores
item.description: You’re welcome.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: Don DeLillo in 1997.

item.title: Chuck Norris Punched This Article Into the Sun: How an Action Hero Became a Meme
item.description: Norris, best known as the butt-kicking star of action films, became an unwitting if good-natured pioneer of the internet meme.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:description: Chuck Norris in “Missing in Action” (1984). He once beat the sun in a staring contest, according to an internet meme from the mid-2000s.

item.title: ‘Dhurandhar the Revenge’ Review: A License to Kill, a Lot
item.description: In this ultraviolent rah-rah sequel, Ranveer Singh returns as an undercover Indian agent who’s part political gangster, part antiterrorist killing machine.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: Bernard Rands, Pulitzer-Winning Composer, Dies at 92
item.description: His early successes in the 1960s, writing in the complex vein of high modernism, yielded later in his career to a more accessibly lyrical style.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Evening Standard/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Rands in 1971. He evolved into a composer whose music was compared by critics to that of Debussy, with shimmering orchestral textures and hints of tonality.

item.title: Lights, Drones, Purple Pizza: BTS-mania Takes Over Seoul
item.description: The South Korean capital was aglow for the return of the K-pop supergroup.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:description: Drones form the image of Jin, a BTS member, in a light show over Seoul on Friday.

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include burned-out paramedics, unlikely superheroes and vengeful women.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: ‘Every Brilliant Thing,’ ‘The Wild Party’ and More Shows Our Critics Enjoyed
item.description: Daniel Radcliffe in “Every Brilliant Thing,” “The Wild Party” and two Cold War-era comedy-thrillers: These are productions worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dulany
item.category: Sexual Misconduct of the Middle Classes (Play)
What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: Daniel Radcliffe in the Broadway play “Every Brilliant Thing” at the Hudson Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Chuck Norris, Action Star of ‘Walker, Texas Ranger,’ Dies at 86
item.description: He channeled his martial arts skills into heroic roles in films like “The Delta Force” and “Missing in Action” and in the long-running TV series “Walker, Texas Ranger.”
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Chuck Norris in 1995 as Cordell Walker on “Walker, Texas Ranger.” The series aired on CBS from 1993 to 2001.

item.title: Calvin Tomkins, Who Profiled Giants of Modern Art, Dies at 100
item.description: On the staff of The New Yorker for more than 60 years, he wrote about Duchamp, Rauschenberg and many others. His books include “Living Well Is the Best Revenge.”
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:credit: Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
item.media:description: Calvin Tomkins at his home in Manhattan. A longtime staff writer at The New Yorker, he specialized in writing profiles of artists.

item.title: Two Revivals, ‘Tru’ and ‘The Fever,’ Tackle the Contagion of the Rich
item.description: Two monologue revivals — Jesse Tyler Ferguson as Truman Capote and Wallace Shawn’s solo — reveal how wealth warps our perceptions. Only one pays dividends.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 11 Recap: Chilling Effect
item.description: An unwelcome visit by some rough Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents added more complications to an already stressful day.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: From left, Shabana Azeez, Josell Mariano, Fiona Dourif, JuJu Alexander, Noah Wyle and Ramona DuBarry in a scene from Episode 11 of Season 2 of “The Pitt.”

item.title: ‘Jury Duty’ Returns. Can Prank Lightning Strike Twice?
item.description: The Amazon comedy, which surrounds an unwitting protagonist with professional actors, returns with a new season that takes place at a company retreat.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.category: Jury Duty Presents: Company Retreat (TV Program)

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come (Movie)

item.title: Five Free Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Payback is bloody, according to this month’s selections, which include Park Chan-wook’s Vengeance Trilogy.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: Spring Is Here. Do You Care?
item.description: A season celebrated by Shakespeare and the Beatles now ranks behind fall in popularity. After a harsh winter, can it regain its former glory?
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Kristian Thacker for The New York Times
item.media:description: Can these little snowdrops compete with maple trees in full autumnal splendor?

item.title: 9 Art Shows to Catch Before They Close This Spring
item.description: Among the must-see exhibitions on view for a limited time are ones featuring a rare Caravaggio, streetscapes covered in orchids and Gabriele Münter’s colorful figures.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.category: Museum of the City of New York
Pioneer Works Center for Art and Innovation (Brooklyn, NY)
item.media:credit: Succession Wifredo Lam, Adagp, Paris/ARS, NY; Guarionex Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wifredo Lam’s “La Jungla (The Jungle)” (1942-43), which is on view at the Museum of Modern Art through April 11 in the retrospective “When I Don’t Sleep, I Dream.”

item.title: ‘Lumière, le Cinéma!’ From Thierry Frémaux
item.description: Directed by Thierry Frémaux, the Cannes chief, the movie argues that filmmaking is an act of faith in humanity.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Late Night Pans Trump’s Pearl Harbor Joke
item.description: “Let me tell you: There is no doubt in my mind that everything he knows about Pearl Harbor begins and ends with a movie starring Ben Affleck,” Jimmy Kimmel said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: A.I. Is Writing Fiction. Publishers Are Unprepared.
item.description: Book publishing has few safeguards in place to prevent the unwitting publication of a novel heavily generated by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Horror Novel ‘Shy Girl’ Canceled Over Suspected A.I. Use
item.description: Its publisher, Hachette, will not release the novel in the United States and will discontinue its U.K. edition, citing its commitment to “original creative expression and storytelling.”
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: The cover of the U.K. edition of “Shy Girl,” by Mia Ballard, which was published by Hachette last year.

item.title: Monopoly? Not a Chance, Says Live Nation’s Chief at Antitrust Trial.
item.description: Michael Rapino, the chief executive of the live entertainment colossus, fought back against accusations his company unfairly dominates the music industry.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Michael Rapino, the chief executive of Live Nation, defended the company from accusations that it is a monopoly that does not care about its customers.

item.title: Thomas Gentille, Artist Who Made Wearable Sculpture, Dies at 89
item.description: He was a master jeweler, but his pieces looked more like miniature contemporary artworks than anything you’d find at Cartier.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:description: Thomas Gentille in 1980. “I think you can express everything in jewelry,” he said. “And that’s what I’m trying to do.”

item.title: ‘Bachelorette’ Season With Taylor Frankie Paul Canceled After Assault Video
item.description: Taylor Frankie Paul, who was set to star in the TV show, had pleaded guilty to aggravated assault after an encounter with her partner in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and John Koblin
item.media:description: Taylor Frankie Paul, right, in a preview for her season of “The Bachelorette.”

item.title: Review: ‘The Wild Party’ Has a Ball at City Center
item.description: Encores! revisits a Jazz Age tale of debauchery, with showstoppers from Jasmine Amy Rogers, Adrienne Warren, Jordan Donica, Tonya Pinkins and others.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Encores! Great American Musicals in Concert (Series)
item.media:description: Adrienne Warren as Kate, left, and Jasmine Amy Rogers as Queenie in the Encores! staging of “The Wild Party” at New York City Center in Manhattan.

item.title: Jane Lapotaire, 81, Actress Hailed Across the Atlantic for ‘Piaf,’ Dies
item.description: A distinguished British stage veteran, she won an Olivier award in London and a Tony in New York for her dramatic portrayal of the French singer Édith Piaf.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Jane Lapotaire in “Piaf” in a 1979 London production. She later made her Broadway debut in the role. Frank Rich wrote in The Times that her performance “burns with such heart-stopping intensity that one never questions her right to stand in for ‘the little sparrow.’”

item.title: Brooklyn Academy of Music Names New President: Tamara McCaw
item.description: Tamara McCaw, a longtime arts leader with experience at the Brooklyn Academy of Music, will lead it with a focus on stability.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:description: Tamara McCaw, the new president of the Brooklyn Academy of Music.

item.title: Pop Mart and Sony Announce Labubu Movie Directed by Paul King
item.description: Pop Mart and Sony announced on Thursday that they were developing a feature film about the fuzzy trinkets. “It’s completely ruthless marketing,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: A performer wearing a Labubu costume greeting visitors at the Pop Land theme park in Beijing last year.

item.title: ‘Miroirs No. 3’ Review: Kindness and Its Reverse Image
item.description: In the lovely new movie from the acclaimed German director Christian Petzold (“Barbara”), a woman wakes to life after an accident.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: At the 34th Outsider Art Fair, Still Genuine Surprises
item.description: Cosmic explosions, proto-Surrealism and names to remember — like the D.J. Raul Hardie and Anne Brown, the high point of our critic’s survey.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Dana Golan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Works by the Gullah artist Sam Doyle at the Gallery of Everything Booth at the Outsider Art Fair in Manhattan. Using house paint on found tin and board, Doyle captured his subjects with graphic confidence.

item.title: New Museum Debuts New Building With Ambitious ‘New Humans’
item.description: It’s a big, serious, adult show worth debating and even fighting over — just the way our critic likes it.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago and Elliott Jerome Brown Jr.

item.title: Crimson Desert Is South Korea’s Latest Game With Big Ambitions
item.description: The open-world fantasy Crimson Desert looks like the latest example of South Korea’s evolution into a gaming powerhouse.
item.dc:creator: Lewis Gordon

item.title: 2 Chainz, One of Hip-Hop’s Cheekiest Lyricists, Gets Serious
item.description: The rapper known for his quirky turns of phrase and malapropisms is trying his hand at a memoir.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.category: The Voice in My Head Is God (Book)
item.media:credit: Alexander J. Rotondo for The New York Times
